runs.interpretation: Secondary-earner substitution-response elasticity used in a U.S. microsimulation model (tax-benefit simulation context)
citations: CBO (Congressional Budget Office) labor supply elasticity assumptions used in microsimulation models
Eissa and Hoynes (2004) — estimated labor supply elasticities for married women
Blundell and MaCurdy (1999) — survey of labor supply elasticities
runs.reasoningSummary: Secondary earners (predominantly married women) are widely documented to have higher labor supply elasticities than primary earners. The empirical literature (Eissa, Hoynes, Blundell, MaCurdy) consistently finds substitution elasticities for married women in the range of 0.2–0.5 on the intensive margin, with some estimates higher when extensive margin responses are included. U.S. microsimulation models such as those used by CBO or TRIM typically calibrate secondary-earner substitution elasticities in the neighborhood of 0.2–0.4. I center my belief at 0.3, reflecting a moderate consensus value, with uncertainty spanning roughly 0.1 to 0.6 to account for variation across model specifications and estimation strategies.

The empirical literature (Eissa, Hoynes, Blundell, MaCurdy) consistently finds substitution elasticities for married women in the range of 0.2\u20130.5 on the intensive margin, with some estimates higher when extensive margin responses are included. U.S. microsimulation models such as those used by CBO or TRIM typically calibrate secondary-earner substitution elasticities in the neighborhood of 0.2\u20130.4. I center my belief at 0.3, reflecting a moderate consensus value, with uncertainty spanning roughly 0.1 to 0.6 to account for variation across model specifications and estimation strategies."}

runs.interpretation: Secondary-earner substitution-response elasticity used in a U.S. microsimulation model (response coefficient on the bounded relative change in effective marginal wage for secondary earners).
citations: CBO (Congressional Budget Office) labor supply elasticity assumptions used in their microsimulation models, roughly 0.2–0.4 for secondary earners
Eissa and Hoynes (2004) estimates of married women's labor supply elasticities
Keane (2011) survey of labor supply elasticities suggesting secondary earners have substitution elasticities in the 0.2–0.5 range
runs.reasoningSummary: Secondary earners (predominantly married women) are widely recognized to have higher labor supply elasticities than primary earners. The empirical literature generally places the substitution (compensated wage) elasticity for secondary earners in the range of 0.2 to 0.5. U.S. microsimulation models such as those used by CBO typically calibrate this parameter in the neighborhood of 0.2–0.4. I center my belief at 0.30, reflecting a moderate consensus value, with uncertainty spanning from about 0.10 (if the model uses a more conservative/recent estimate reflecting declining elasticities over time) to 0.60 (if the model adopts a higher traditional estimate).

runs.interpretation: Secondary-earner substitution-response elasticity used in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: CBO effective marginal tax rate and labor supply microsimulation documentation
Eissa and Hoynes (2004) on married women labor supply elasticities
TRIM3 / Urban Institute tax-benefit microsimulation parameterization
runs.reasoningSummary: Secondary earners (predominantly married women) are widely found to have higher labor supply elasticities than primary earners. The empirical literature on married women's wage elasticity of hours/participation typically centers around 0.2–0.5, with many estimates near 0.3. U.S. tax-benefit microsimulation models (e.g., CBO's labor supply model, TRIM3, and similar tools) typically adopt a substitution elasticity for secondary earners in the range of 0.2–0.4, often around 0.3. This reflects the consensus from the quasi-experimental and structural estimation literature. I center my belief at 0.30 with moderate uncertainty spanning roughly 0.10 to 0.60 to account for variation across model implementations and parameter calibration choices.

runs.interpretation: Secondary-earner substitution-response elasticity used in a U.S. microsimulation model (tax-benefit simulation context)
citations: CBO (Congressional Budget Office) effective marginal tax rate and labor supply modeling documentation
Eissa and Hoynes (2004) - labor supply responses of married women to tax changes
Hausman (1985) and Blundell/MaCurdy labor supply elasticity surveys
runs.reasoningSummary: Secondary earners (typically married women) are widely understood to have higher labor supply elasticities than primary earners. The empirical literature anchors the substitution elasticity for this group roughly in the 0.2–0.5 range. U.S. microsimulation models such as those used by CBO or the Urban-Brookings Tax Policy Center typically parameterize the secondary-earner substitution elasticity around 0.2–0.4. A value near 0.3 is a common calibration choice, balancing older estimates (Hausman-era, often higher ~0.4–0.5) with more recent quasi-experimental estimates that tend to be somewhat lower (~0.2–0.3). I center my belief at 0.3 with moderate uncertainty reflecting variation across model implementations and the underlying empirical evidence.

runs.interpretation: Secondary-earner substitution-response elasticity used in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: CBO effective marginal tax rate and labor supply microsimulation documentation
Eissa and Hoynes (2004) on married women labor supply elasticities
TAXSIM/NBER microsimulation parameterization literature
runs.reasoningSummary: Secondary earners (predominantly married women) have historically been found to have higher labor supply elasticities than primary earners. The empirical literature on married women's wage elasticity of labor supply on the intensive margin clusters around 0.2–0.4, with older studies finding higher values and more recent studies finding somewhat lower values as female labor force participation has risen. U.S. tax-benefit microsimulation models (e.g., CBO's labor supply model, TRIM, or similar) typically parameterize the secondary-earner substitution elasticity in the range of 0.2–0.4. A value around 0.3 is a common central calibration. I place my median at 0.30 with moderate uncertainty reflecting variation across model vintages and calibration choices.
